metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/_lib_reads.py` and `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` as external callers of `fsck_cli` that must be verified to remain correct when `run_ensures()` is wired into the fsck path.
findings.finding: The plan tests `run_ensures()` wired into `fsck_cli` (via the no_mutate and healing-behavior ACs), but does not enumerate the external callers of `fsck_cli` that live outside `src/rebar/_commands/`: `src/rebar/_lib_reads.py` (line 274: `_fsck_mod.fsck_cli([], repo_root=repo_root, no_mutate=report_only)`) and `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (line 260: `return _fsck.fsck_cli(rest)`). If `run_ensures()` is wired into `fsck_cli`, these callers silently acquire the new behavior without acknowledgment.

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether the concurrency AC test should extend `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` (noted 'WS5c' hook) or add a separate test, and reference the existing `_engine_run`/`_make_repo` harness so implementers don't duplicate it.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC ('two processes sweeping the same checkout') does not reference the existing `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py` harness, which already implements a real two-process write/lock/converge pattern using `_engine_run` + real OS processes. The plan risks duplicating this infrastructure rather than extending it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare WS1 and WS2 as stated pre-requisites in the Scope section, and add a note specifying whether WS5 tests should be written against WS1/WS2 stubs/mocks or only authored once those workstreams are merged.
findings.finding: The primary symbols under test — `run_ensures()`, `applied_set`, `.ensure-applied` marker, `.ensure-hinted` marker, and `ensure.hint_enabled` config key — do not exist anywhere in the codebase (zero matches in src/ or tests/). They are deliverables from WS1 and WS2 that have not yet landed. The plan never explicitly declares WS1/WS2 as pre-requisites, so an implementer has no stated gate before which these tests cannot compile or run.
findings.location: What section; Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope section: 'Pre-requisites: WS1 (run_ensures, ensures.py, .ensure-applied) and WS2 (.ensure-hinted, pending-hint machinery) must be merged before WS5 tests can execute. Tests may be authored against the interfaces specified in WS1/WS2 plans but should use pytest.importorskip or a stub fixture if running before those branches land.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title to replace 'staleness-hint' with 'pending-hint' to match the WS2-consistent terminology stated in the Why section and used in the ACs.
findings.finding: The plan title uses the term 'staleness-hint' but the Why section explicitly states this is wrong: 'this is the "pending-hint", consistent with WS2 — not "staleness-hint".' The AC then correctly uses 'Pending-hint tests'. This creates a cross-section terminology contradiction between the title and the body/AC.
findings.location: Plan title vs. Why section vs. AC line 3 ('Pending-hint tests')
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title to: 'WS5: tests — idempotency, hot-path regression, pending-hint, concurrency, no_mutate, absent-marker'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace all references to `fsck --fix` in the Scope and What sections with `fsck --repair`, matching the actual CLI flag confirmed in `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out`.
findings.finding: The Scope section names 'server/fsck --fix healing behavior' and the What section describes 'the server/fsck --fix healing behavior' as in-scope. However, `--fix` is not a valid `rebar fsck` flag — the actual mutating flag is `--repair` (confirmed by `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` line 1 and grep hits for `fsck --repair` in integration tests). This is a hallucinated CLI flag name in the test scope description.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'server/fsck --fix healing behavior' → 'server/fsck --repair healing behavior' and update the no_mutate AC description to reference `--repair` (or the `no_mutate=True` kwarg path in `fsck_cli`) as appropriate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Why or Scope section explaining why a dedicated behavioral test workstream (WS5) separated from feature code (WS1-3) is the right approach — e.g., that concurrency and hot-path invariants require OS-level process isolation not available within unit test boundaries, and that separation keeps feature PRs reviewable.
findings.finding: The plan's Why section provides outcome rationale ('Only tests lock the invariants so future ensures can be added safely') but states no positive rationale for WHY the chosen test approach — behavioral end-to-end assertions via subprocess/monkeypatch in a dedicated WS5 workstream — is the right fit for these specific invariants, as opposed to embedding the tests alongside the feature code in WS1-3. The absence of approach rationale is a G6 finding (MINOR).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the instrumentation strategy for the two sub-claims in the hot-path AC: name the mock/patch target for the git-config subprocess assertion (e.g., `unittest.mock.patch('subprocess.run')` / `subprocess.Popen`) and the mechanism for counting `.ensure-applied` reads (e.g., patch `builtins.open` with a counter fixture).
findings.finding: The hot-path AC asserts 'spawns no git-config subprocess' and 'reads `.ensure-applied` at most once per process', but the plan names no instrumentation strategy (e.g., mock/patch the subprocess layer, wrap `open()`, use an in-process counter) that would let an executor write a deterministic assertion. Without a stated approach, different executors may reach inconsistent test implementations or silently skip the hardest claim.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Hot-path regression test bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note such as: 'Assert subprocess.run/Popen call count == 0 via `unittest.mock.patch`; assert open-call count on `.ensure-applied` == 1 via a patched `builtins.open` counter fixture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an implementation note to the concurrency AC specifying the proving approach for atomicity (e.g., 'verify implementation uses write-to-temp-then-rename; test by patching `os.rename` to assert it is called, or by spawning a concurrent reader in a tight loop and asserting it never observes a partial/corrupt payload').
findings.finding: The concurrency AC claims `.ensure-applied` is written atomically with 'no torn/lost state', but provides no proving approach for the torn-write assertion. Torn-write correctness in tests typically requires either a fault-injection hook (pause writer mid-write, assert reader never sees a partial file) or a statistical concurrent-read/write loop with content validation. Without this, an executor has no guidance on how to distinguish 'untorn in practice' from 'untorn by construction'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default rate-limit interval value and its config key name in the pending-hint AC, and specify how the test overrides it (e.g., 'set `ensure.hint_interval_seconds=1` in the test fixture and advance a mock clock, or freeze time with `freezegun`').
findings.finding: The pending-hint AC references 'rate-limited to the configured interval' but the plan never defines what that interval value is, nor where it is declared (config key name, default value, test fixture value). An executor writing the rate-limit test must invent or hunt for this value, which could produce a test that passes vacuously (interval=0) or one that requires wall-clock sleep (interval=60s).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Pending-hint tests bullet; also Why section ('rate-limited via `.ensure-hinted` interval')
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Rate-limit interval is controlled by `ensure.hint_interval_seconds` (default: 3600). Tests MUST override this via a mock clock or `freezegun`; wall-clock sleep is not acceptable in CI.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'reads `.ensure-applied` at most once per process' with a behavioral observable (e.g. a wall-clock or syscall-count regression bound using a real filesystem shim, or a throughput assertion) so the hot-path regression test does not degrade into a mock-call-count change-detector.
findings.finding: The hot-path regression AC includes 'reads `.ensure-applied` at most once per process' as a sub-criterion. This is an internal call-count detail, not an observable behavioral property. Implementing it almost certainly requires either (a) mocking/spying on file-I/O and asserting call counts — a change-detector anti-pattern that locks in internal structure — or (b) a source-grep of the implementation, which is explicitly a prohibited proxy. Neither approach validates the behavioral property (hot-path callers don't pay repeated I/O cost); a latency/throughput assertion or a filesystem-layer shim that counts real OS calls and is decoupled from the implementation's internal structure would be behavioral.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Hot-path regression test bullet: 'reads `.ensure-applied` at most once per process'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the call-count criterion with an end-to-end latency/throughput bound: run N hot-path mutations under a real (tmpfs) worktree and assert total elapsed time stays below a threshold, OR use an OS-level strace/dtrace hook in the test harness to count actual `open(2)` calls on the specific path — both approaches remain behavioral and decouple from implementation internals.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the instrumentation strategy for verifying 'spawns no git-config subprocess' in AC2 — e.g., monkeypatch `subprocess.run` and assert call count, or inject a test-only subprocess spy — so the assertion is non-vacuous and CI-compatible.
findings.finding: The hot-path regression AC asserts 'spawns no git-config subprocess' but the plan states no instrumentation strategy for observing subprocess spawn count — this is a non-trivial mechanism gap because `_seam.author()` unconditionally launches `git config user.name` via `subprocess.run` on every commit path, and verifying it is never called requires either patching `subprocess.run`, a call-count spy, or an env-level trap. Without naming the approach, an implementer may write an untestable or vacuously-green assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'Verified by monkeypatching `subprocess.run` (or `rebar._commands._seam.author`) and asserting the patched call is never reached during the hot-path mutation; the patch must remain scoped to the test to avoid polluting other assertions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the subprocess-count instrumentation strategy for the hot-path regression test (e.g., monkeypatch `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` and assert call count ≤ 0 for `git config` invocations, or use a `CountingSpy` fixture) so implementers don't each invent a different approach.
findings.finding: The hot-path AC asserts 'spawns no git-config subprocess' as a verifiable invariant, but the plan names no instrumentation mechanism and there is zero prior art in the test suite for counting subprocess spawns (no `subprocess_count`, no patch-and-count pattern exists anywhere in tests/).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the hot-path AC: 'Instrument by monkeypatching `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` (the sole git subprocess wrapper) with a recording spy; assert no call whose args contain "config" fires during the hot path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `.ensure-hinted` follows the existing sync-throttle convention (`/tmp/.ticket-sync-<hash>` pattern) or is stored inside the tracker dir, and confirm the test harness can expire it deterministically (as `_expire_sync_marker` does for the sync throttle).
findings.finding: The pending-hint rate-limit test implicitly assumes a new `.ensure-hinted` file-based throttle, but the plan does not acknowledge the existing sync-throttle pattern (`/tmp/.ticket-sync-<md5(realpath(tracker))[:12]>`) already in the codebase, nor does it clarify whether the new hint throttle should follow the same tmpfile-per-tracker-realpath convention or use a different location/format.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Pending-hint tests; What section ('rate-limited via `.ensure-hinted` interval')

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
